Is every day WTI crude oil stays above $65 still a great day?

Oil Prices Retreat to Pre-War Level as Supply Concerns Ease

Crude oil prices and calendar spreads have largely reverted to pre-war levels as traders anticipate a rapid normalisation of exports through the Strait of Hormuz and the smooth replenishment of depleted oil inventories around the world.

Front-month Brent crude futures retreated to $77 per barrel on June 23, down from a wartime high of $126 and the lowest since the United States and Israel launched a surprise attack on Iran at the end of February.

Prices have retreated fastest for contracts nearest to expiry causing the backwardation to soften and the futures curve to flatten ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to clients earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/YjJTctVocX

This is my analysis of the Strait of Hormuz and you can see it in the video from @MarineTraffic. 

Tanker inflows remain heavily restricted. I have included a table of June 24 data thus far.

What's obvious to me is that there's an exodus of tankers using the Oman route. You can see this at the beginning of the video and towards the end of the video. There's another group ready to leave.

The US appears to be coordinating with these groups to support transit. 

Again, most of the traffic is outbound as they are trying to get stranded tankers out.

IRGC just announced that tankers must adhere to the rules. But so far, there's not much enforcement on the Oman lane. The longer the transits continue on the Oman lane, the faster IRGC will lose control of the Strait. This means that escalation is a high probability scenario.

Key parts of the oil market are suddenly awash in supply, as a stream of cargoes out of the Strait of Hormuz accelerates after the US-Iran agreement to open the waterway. - Bloomberg

EIA inventory report:

U.S. commercial crude inventories fell by 6 million barrels, while the Strategic Petroleum Reserve declined by a further 9 million barrels to a fresh multi-decade low. Combined, total U.S. crude stockpiles have fallen by around 100 million barrels since late April.
Inventories at Cushing, Oklahoma, the delivery hub for WTI futures, dropped to just 19 million barrels, the lowest level in 12 years and a threshold increasingly associated with operational constraints.
Gasoline and distillate inventories rose by 2 million and 3 million barrels, respectively, supported by robust refinery utilization and softer demand. Four-week average implied gasoline demand fell to the lowest seasonal level in 12 years, excluding the pandemic-affected period in 2020.
Total crude and refined product exports slowed to 12.1 million barrels per day, while U.S. crude production held steady at 13.8 million barrels per day.
Oil prices reset as stranded Gulf barrels head for market https://t.co/QG50uLoja3 via @saxobank

Trump is now turning his attention to refiners and fuel producers, criticizing the slow pace of declines in retail gasoline prices following the sharp drop in crude oil. However, the refining sector's response remains constrained by historically low inventories of refined products, particularly in the middle distillate complex.

As the charts highlight, US gasoline crack spreads remain elevated despite the recent easing in crude prices. The biggest beneficiary of the reopening of Middle East export flows has so far been the distillate market, with gasoil and diesel cracks falling from their March peaks. This reflects the fact that much of the additional crude now reaching the market is medium-to-heavy Gulf crude, which yields a higher proportion of middle distillates such as diesel, gasoil and jet fuel.

While increased crude availability should eventually help ease refining margins and lower fuel prices, depleted product inventories accumulated during the Strait of Hormuz disruption continue to support cracks. The combination of peak summer demand for gasoline keeping prices at the pumps seasonally elevated, refiners – for now - remain incentivized to maximize diesel and jet fuel production, delaying a more pronounced decline in gasoline prices.

text:
Daily Digest-Night Update 6/24

📷DOW NASDAQ S&P 500 RUSSELL 2000

US stocks ended mixed as tech and semiconductor weakness persisted while falling oil and Fed stress test results supported cyclicals and financials ahead of key inflation and growth data

The Dow closed higher while the S&P 500 and Nasdaq extended their losing streak to a third session amid continued pressure on growth stocks tied to AI valuation concerns and earlier chip sector selling.

Tech and semiconductor names lagged, with Qualcomm (QCOM), Sandisk (SNDK) and the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) edging lower, while Cerebras (CBRS) fell after issuing a disappointing post-IPO margin outlook.
Energy stocks weakened as crude (@CL) prices slid toward prewar levels on increased Middle East supply, while lower oil costs supported airlines and travel names and eased inflation expectations.
Financials advanced after major banks including JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Goldman Sachs (GS) passed the Federal Reserve’s annual stress test, announced share repurchases and dividend hikes, and were deemed able to withstand large losses.
Sunrun (RUN) rose sharply after striking a deal with Tesla (TSLA) to supply power for AI data centers, making it one of the session’s strongest gainers.
Wendy’s (WEN) jumped following a Reddit-driven meme rally focused on its high short interest and the hiring of a new CFO, with heavy retail trading activity pushing the stock intraday to multi-month highs.
Homebuilders such as KB Home (KBH) gained after passage of an affordable housing bill, lifting the group on expectations of increased demand and supportive policy tailwinds.
After the close, Micron (MU) reported Q3 results that beat revenue and EPS expectations and issued strong AI-driven Q4 guidance, sending the shares significantly higher in after-hours trading following earlier intraday weakness.
Traders are looking ahead to Thursday’s PCE inflation report, a GDP revision and weekly jobless claims for further clarity on the Fed’s policy path, along with market follow-through from Micron (MU)’s earnings and other corporate results.
https://t.co/EH4FbiAMoN

text:
Good Morning! 

Futures are up, with ES +27

Oil prices are lower this morning, with WTI crude down to $71 a barrel. 

The techs are looking better this morning. 

South Korea's KOSPI regained ground after yesterday's tech driven sell-off. 

The market is waiting on $MU earnings which will drop later today after the bell. This report will be a critical indicator of semiconductor demand and AI spending. The market can see a big move in the AI tech stocks, led by chips tomorrow. 

$NVDA is leading the Mag 7 this morning. 

$GOOGL will join the Dow Jones Industrial Average, replacing Verizon $VZ, effective Monday, June 29, expanding mega cap tech presence in the blue chip average.

$TWLO is up 4% pre-market on renewed analyst optimism, including a Goldman Sachs Buy initiation with a $300 target, building on the their raised 2026 outlook and AI platform momentum following a successful turnaround.

The AI chip supply chain is facing a critical bottleneck at the manufacturing and testing layer, where optical interconnect scaling and silicon photonics capacity are emerging as key constraints on AI data center buildout. This is bullish for $ALAB $CRDO $AAOI $CIEN $COHR $FN $GLW $LITE. 

I don't see any other notable pre-market activity or significant news. 

I expect to see very little action in the market ahead of $MU earnings later today. 

The market may trade flat and range bound with very little volume for most of the day, with some movement at the end of the day as people do some last minute positioning ahead of those earnings.

US Stocks Set to Recover After Sharp Tech Selloff as Focus Shifts to Micron Earnings

U.S. stock futures are pointing higher Wednesday morning as markets look to stabilize following Tuesday’s sharp tech-led decline. The selloff was driven by weakness in semiconductor and big tech stocks, but investors are now turning their attention to $MU’s upcoming earnings.

Futures Snapshot:
- E-Mini S&P 500 up 0.25% at $7455.75
- E-Mini Nasdaq 100 up 0.58% at $29838.00
- E-Mini Dow down 0.03% at $52064.00

Oil Prices:
- WTI Crude down 1.86% near $71.85
- Brent Crude down 1.69% at $75.50

Key Market Context:
Markets are attempting a recovery after a difficult Tuesday session in which chip stocks and several Magnificent Seven names came under heavy pressure. The rebound in futures is being supported by bargain hunting ahead of $MU’s third-quarter results, which are due after the market close and are seen as a major test for the AI and memory chip trade.

Premarket Movers:
- $MU is up nearly 4% as investors position ahead of earnings. The stock had dropped more than 13% on Tuesday.
- Cerebras Systems is down more than 14% in premarket despite beating expectations in its first earnings report as a public company.
- FedEx is down around 7% after delivering mixed results and cautious full-year guidance.
- Twilio is up 5% after Goldman Sachs initiated coverage with a Buy rating and a $300 price target.
- Wendy’s is surging nearly 14% amid retail investor interest and short-squeeze speculation.

Asia Market Update:
South Korea’s Kospi Index is rebounding strongly after Monday’s roughly 10% plunge. The benchmark rose 3.26% on Wednesday as local investors bought the dip in technology stocks. The Kospi remains one of the best-performing major indices globally this year, up more than 100%.

Economic Calendar:
Today’s key data releases include:
- U.S. new home sales for May (10:00 a.m. ET)
- U.S. leading economic indicators for May (10:00 a.m. ET)
- Weekly crude oil inventories (10:30 a.m. ET)
- $70 billion 5-year Treasury note auction result (1:00 p.m. ET)

Overall Market View:
Sentiment is showing signs of stabilization after Tuesday’s tech rout, with investors shifting focus toward $MU’s earnings as a potential catalyst for the semiconductor and AI sectors. While the broader market remains cautious, the combination of oversold conditions in some tech names and expectations around $MU’s results is supporting a modest recovery in futures. Markets will be watching closely to see whether $MU can deliver strong enough results to restore confidence in the AI trade.

WTI crude oil taps early-March lows near $71/bbl. The shipping recovery in the Strait of Hormuz and slowing global demand are crushing any rebound momentum. 

🟢 Goldman Sachs (The Moderate):
Expects WTI to target ~$75 by Q4, with a full-year average range of $70–$78. They see high-level volatility persisting as geopolitical wildcards linger.

🔴 JPMorgan (The Bear):
Sticking firmly to its bearish outlook, forecasting a major slide down to $55–$58.

🐦 Z Pulse

• Oil Collapse: Brent crude falls to $75, lowest since February. Trump orders DOJ probe into oil companies for price manipulation while prices crater. Conflicting narrative on who's inflating what.

• Turkey MSCI Ultimatum: MSCI demands transparency reforms (ownership disclosure, market surveillance) by November 2026 or Turkey faces potential index removal. Heavy accusations per local analysts.

• $MU Earnings Watch: Micron reports today, market in wait-and-see mode. AI chip sector whipsawed after $NVDA AGM and margin concerns at peers like $CBRS. Space stocks ($ASTS, $RKLB) struggling while AI infrastructure attracts capital.

• AI Chip M&A: $QCOM acquires Modular Inc. for ~$4B to enable cross-platform AI software deployment. SK Hynix Nasdaq listing July 10, targeting $30B+ raise for memory chip capacity.

📰 News Flow

• Iran-Dollar Talks: Treasury Secretary Bessent says US-Iran negotiations include Tehran invoicing oil in dollars. Goldman cuts Q4 crude forecast to $80 citing US-Iran interim peace deal. IAEA dismisses "war of words," expects resumed inspections.

• Data Center Power Rush: $META and $MSFT lead $850B boom in data center leases. Multiple partnerships announced: $TSLA/$RUN team on AI data center energy, FuelCell/$FCEL partners with Fit Energy, Canadian nuclear firm AtkinsRealis seeks US approval for AI power tech.

• China Mineral Controls: Beijing detains two Japanese nationals over alleged rare earth smuggling, launches whistleblower hotline for export violations. Escalating minerals trade enforcement amid tech rivalry.

📊 Market Direction Read

• Bias: Cautious — Futures mixed, oil plunging, tech recovering from 3%+ $NDX selloff but awaiting $MU earnings as "gut check" for AI trade.

• Triggers:

• $MU earnings (afternoon) — memory pricing/AI demand signal for entire chip sector
• Oil volatility — Brent at $75, Trump DOJ probe vs. Iran deal conflicting narratives
• DXY above 101 pressuring gold, crypto ($BTC testing 59-60k support)

• Risk: $MU disappointment could reignite AI infrastructure skepticism after margin pressure at $CBRS; oil collapse may signal demand weakness beyond geopolitics.

🎯 On Watch Today

$MU earnings after close. Dan Ives (Wedbush) calls it a "gut check moment" for markets. Memory chip pricing and AI capex outlook will set tone for semiconductor complex heading into month-end.

Goldman Sachs cuts Q4 crude forecast to $80.

→ Gulf oil exports rebound to 60% of pre-war levels.
→ Markets question need for large security premium.
→ EV surge raises doubts on oil demand recovery.

Watch energy stocks, inflation trends, and currency moves.

JP Morgan just cut its Brent crude outlook for late 2026.

→ Q3 forecast: $86/barrel (down from prior)
→ Q4 forecast: $80/barrel
→ Year-end target: $78/barrel
→ Cites weak OECD inventory draws and lower oil demand

The United States’ top diplomat sought backing from skeptical Gulf allies on Wednesday for President Donald Trump’s deal with Iran to end their war, while in another challenge to the accord, Israel insisted it would keep troops in southern Lebanon.

The US and Iran signed an initial accord last week to end a war that has upended the Middle East and  pressured global economies with the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a  transit point for a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas  supplies. Under the deal,  the strait is now slowly reopening and on Wednesday benchmark oil  prices fell more than $3 to their lowest level since before the war  started as supply concerns eased further.

But conflicting accounts have emerged over elements of the deal, which has prompted criticism of Trump at home and in the Middle East. Financial incentives for Iran, control of the Strait of Hormuz, and Israel‘s parallel war in Lebanon have all been disputed, highlighting the fragility of the accord.

“We’re winning by a lot. Iran is making very big concessions,” Trump told reporters on Wednesday.

Speaking in Kuwait City, the second stopover in a tour of three Gulf nations, Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the US was “completely aligned with our partners in the Gulf.”

“We’re not going to do anything that undermines the security of our  allies, our longstanding allies in the region,” he told reporters before  heading to Bahrain.

Rubio also reiterated the Trump administration’s opposition to Iran‘s demand to charge tolls on shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, something also opposed by Washington’s Gulf allies.
Reuters/Algemeiner

🚨 FLASHING RED: THE CRUSH AT CUSHING
The corporate media is completely silent on this, but the system monitors are blinking absolute red. Cushing, Oklahoma, the "Pipeline Crossroads of the World" and the heart of American energy distribution, is running dangerously dry.

Government and industry data just confirmed that commercial crude inventories at the Cushing hub have plummeted for eight consecutive weeks, dropping straight down to 20 million barrels.

In the oil business, 20 million isn't just a low number, it is the absolute operational floor.

Here is what they aren't telling you on the nightly news:

The Suction Line Risk: Dropping below this threshold means hitting "tank bottoms." Pumping crude below this line becomes mechanically hazardous, highly expensive, and risks serious sediment contamination.

The Hidden Drain: While the establishment claims everything is stable, the U.S. has been operating as the "seller of last resort" to the global market, draining domestic hubs to ship record-breaking crude exports overseas.

The 4th of July Threat: We are currently sitting less than two million barrels away from a total operational empty zone. Refineries are running at maximum capacity for summer travel, meaning the domestic distribution cushion has officially been worn paper-thin.

The buffer is completely gone. If one major pipeline or refinery takes a surprise hit between now and the holiday, there is zero safety net left to absorb the shock.

We are tracking the raw mechanics of this engineered infrastructure squeeze behind the scenes. The warnings are live, and the grid is under immense structural stress.

TONIGHT ON KINETIC NEWS DAILY: We are dropping the full, unclassified blueprint on exactly why they are bleeding our reserves, who is hiding the real data, and how to lock down your personal perimeter before the system faces a hard reset.
Stay vigilant. Keep your mind sovereign.
#CushingOklahoma #GlobalEnergyShock #KineticNewsDaily #GridVulnerability #EnergySovereignty
@tylermacro10 Forgot to finish my thought, I think if we got to the point where crude inventories were low that supply + storage couldn’t meet refinery demand then crude would have taken off

This chart is showing the U.S. energy cushion getting stripped down. It combines crude oil, the SPR, gasoline, distillate, and jet fuel, so it is not just a crude inventory chart. It is a picture of the whole liquid fuels buffer. The dark green 2026 line has fallen almost vertically into week 25 and is sitting far below the 5 year pattern. That tells you the system has been drawing heavily on stored energy to absorb the Hormuz shock, refinery demand, exports, and disrupted global flows. The latest EIA data still show refineries running hard at 96.1% utilization, with crude inputs around 17.1 million barrels per day, while total commercial petroleum inventories fell slightly even as gasoline and distillate inventories rose. That mix matters because it says the upstream barrel cushion is tight, but end product demand is not uniformly strong.  

The nuance is that this can coexist with oil prices falling. People think low inventories should automatically mean higher oil, but price is about the next marginal condition. Brent is falling because the market is removing the Hormuz war premium as tanker flows improve and stranded crude starts moving again. Brent settled around $73.74, its lowest since before the Iran war, as supply disruption fears eased. But the inventory chart says the physical buffer is still thin. That means lower oil is not necessarily a clean all clear signal. It may be saying the market is now more worried about demand destruction than immediate supply loss.

This is a late cycle energy tape. The U.S. burned through a lot of cushion to stabilize the shock, but now the price is falling because the economy may not be able to absorb expensive energy anymore. That is not the same as abundance. It is fragility with weaker demand layered on top. If demand improves and inventories rebuild, this becomes a successful shock absorption story. But if oil keeps falling while storage remains depleted, the dollar stays strong, products build, and industrial indicators soften, then the message is darker. It means the market is not pricing comfort. It is pricing a slowdown after the emergency barrels have already been used.

text:
Oil Falls to Postwar Lows
by Bloomberg  C. Charleston, A. Longley, G. Levin  | Wednesday, June 24, 2026
Oil's wartime price gains have evaporated as the US and Iran log progress in peace talks and more tankers openly transit the Strait of Hormuz, easing fears of a supply crunch.

West Texas Intermediate fell to settle near $70 a barrel, the lowest since the end of February just before the US and Israel launched their war on Iran. Brent, the global benchmark, settled below $74 a barrel, also the lowest since the war broke out.

"Oil is dropping sharply as the Strait of Hormuz remains open and traders start to focus on OPEC, including Iran, potentially moving to maximum production," Infrastructure Capital Management chief executive Jay Hatfield said. "We forecast that WTI could drop below $60 over the next two months as production ramps and stocks rebuild."

Vessels are transiting Hormuz with their satellite signals switched on, indicating growing confidence among shipowners about safe passage of the chokepoint, through which about a fifth of global oil supplies transited before the war. The International Maritime Organization also said it had received safety guarantees allowing hundreds of ships to exit the Persian Gulf.

Washington and Tehran have both flagged early progress in talks to end the war, although negotiations are likely to be protracted and claims from the two sides have diverged. In a sign of how much oil has been leaving Hormuz in recent weeks, the International Energy Agency estimates that the United Arab Emirates is exporting oil at nearly 85% of pre-war levels.

The resumed flows out of the Persian Gulf come as US data showed domestic crude inventories plunged to the lowest since 1984. Stockpiles at Cushing, Oklahoma, dropped below 19 million barrels, according to the Energy Information Administration, slipping below levels many traders view as operational minimums. Still, prices were little changed after the release, with traders focusing on broader signs of easing supply tightness.

A closely watched oil market indicator flipped into a bearish structure on Wednesday for the first time since February, with Brent's prompt time-spread trading in a shallow contango, with the nearest contract's price below the next month's. That structure typically signals expectations of oversupply.

"I think the physical markets are much weaker than most people anticipated in Asia and Europe with Brent now going contango as well," TP ICAP analyst Scott Shelton said. "This subtracts buyers from the oil market which makes it fall much more easily."

There's also been a collapse in prices for real-world barrels, with premiums for barrels from the North Sea to West Africa tumbling.

Oil prices are down about 40% from their high during the peak of the conflict, with the steady increase in traffic adding to workarounds that were put in place to mitigate disruption through Hormuz at the height of the war. The UAE alone has sold around 60 million barrels from inside the Persian Gulf in recent weeks.

The Republican-led Senate voted Tuesday to end the US war with Iran in a rare symbolic rebuke of US President Donald Trump. While the resolution is unlikely to force any changes in the administration's strategy, it represents the latest sign that the president lacks domestic support for the effort.

Separately, Trump said in a social media post he had ordered the Department of Justice to look into why gasoline prices haven't fallen faster as oil drops. The national average retail price has declined 14% since late May and is now below $4 a gallon, although it remains above the five-year seasonal average, according to data from the American Automobile Association. US retail diesel prices fell below $5 a gallon for the first time since mid-March.

Oil Falls to Postwar Lows Despite historically low US crude inventories, traders focused on improving Middle East supply flows and growing signs of potential oversupply.

$CL #OOTT

@Nostre_damus Officials promise low gas this summer while U.S. crude inventories sit at their lowest since 1984. The Strait is ‘reopening’ but mine clearance, repairs & ramping production takes months. Analysts say pre-war prices won’t return until 2027-28. The $3 gas talk is pure gaslighting

Welcome to Mid-Week Trade! Wednesday was a mixed day on Wall Street, headlined by big bearish moves in the metals markets. Both gold and silver put in major sell-offs amid a suddenly stronger USD. Key natural support levels bit the dust as metals bidders stayed on the sidelines.

On the economic news front, Wednesday’s key item was the weekly EIA crude oil inventories report. Inventories fell by more than 6 million barrels for the week and now stand 7% below the seasonally-adjusted five-year average. In addition, the United States Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) is currently at its lowest level since 1983. In short, US oil supplies are down dramatically as a result of the war in Iran.

It’s been a brutal June for #gold. The market has finally broken out of a 60-day consolidation period and is on the run south beneath $4000. Here’s a support zone worth watching for the coming weeks in the #XAUUSD:

Monthly 62% Retracement, 3706.28
Monthly Bollinger Mid-Point, 3703.34

Check out our Key Levels for more information on this Trade Idea.

At press time, the MoU between Iran and the US is holding firm. We’ll have the latest on the situation tomorrow at 1:00 PM EST over at Switch Markets on YouTube. See you there!

text: $USO burning through oil right now EIA Crude Oil Inventories Actual -6.088M

U.S. SESSION: HOUSING CRACKS, OIL TANKS DESPITE SUPPLY SQUEEZE

May new home sales plunged 7.3% to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 580,000 units – well below the 638,000 forecast and down from April's upwardly revised 626,000.

The median new home price held at $424,900, but the average price rose 5.0% year-over-year. Prices aren't falling – buyers just can't afford them.

🛢️ EIA CRUDE INVENTORIES: BEAT THE ESTIMATE, BUT OIL STILL CRASHED

Headline: 
Crude inventories fell -6.1M barrels (forecast -3.9M, previous -8.3M). Total commercial stocks dropped to 412.1M barrels – about 7% below the 5-year average. 

Strategic reserves fell to 331.2M barrels – the lowest since 1983. Cushing inventories hit their lowest since October 2014.

Yet WTI crashed over 4.67% to ~$70, breaking below the $70 level.

The disconnect: 
Refineries are processing oil at record rates, and exports remain elevated. But gasoline inventories rose +2.064M barrels (forecast -0.578M) and distillates rose +3.064M barrels (forecast -0.505M). 

Finished products are piling up faster than consumers can use them – suggesting weaker domestic demand.

The bigger driver: 
the U.S.-Iran peace deal has unwound the wartime risk premium. The IMO has confirmed safety guarantees for tankers crossing the Strait of Hormuz. WTI is now trading roughly 40% below its conflict peak.

$WTI $USO $XHB $SPY $TLT

quotedTweet.text:
It’s a breakdown of the key Asian session economic data releases from the table (Japan & Australia):

🇯🇵 Japan – SPPI y/y (Services PPI)

· Actual: 3.3% (in line with forecasts)

· Previous: 3.4% (revised down slightly)

· Takeaway: No surprise here. The slight dip from prior isn't large enough to shift BoJ policy expectations, so JPY reaction is muted.

🇦🇺 Australia – Mixed but mostly weaker data

1. Retail Sales m/m (implied by the -0.7% figure)
   · Actual: -0.7% (sharp miss vs -0.4% forecast)
   · Previous: +0.4%
   · Takeaway: A significant contraction in consumer spending. This is bearish for AUD.

2. Monthly CPI y/y (table labels it "CPI m/m")
 
· Actual: 4.0% (below 4.3% forecast & below 4.2% previous)

· Takeaway: Headline inflation cooled more than expected.

3. Quarterly CPI y/y
· Actual: 4.0% (below 4.3% forecast & down from 4.4% previous)

· Takeaway: Confirms disinflationary trends, reducing RBA rate-hike pressure.

4. Trimmed Mean CPI q/q (core inflation)
· Actual: 0.4% (beat 0.3% forecast; previous 0.3%)

· Takeaway: Core inflation ticked up slightly, offering a tiny offset to the weak headline.

Overall Session Sentiment:

· AUD is under pressure – weak Retail Sales combined with softer headline CPI outweigh the slight core beat, reinforcing dovish RBA expectations.

· JPY is neutral – the SPPI print changed nothing.

In FX, expect AUDJPY to face downside risks following this mix.

text: API crude inventories fell 6.1MM barrels while gasoline and distillates rose, production climbed 13k bbl/d to 13.8MM, and SPR dropped to 331.2MM barrels—lowest since 1983.

Cushing down to below 19mil barrels week-to-date

Update after the release:

EIA crude came in with a bigger draw than expected.

Crude:
-6.1M vs -3.6M expected

But products built:
Gasoline +2.1M
Distillates +3.1M

So the headline supports crude, but the signal is not perfectly clean.

Now the important part:
watch the oil reaction, not just the number.

If oil still stays heavy after a bigger crude draw, relief is still controlling the tape.

#RUSSIA 🇷🇺

✅ Russia's oil exports from its western ports are expected to hit a record 2.7 million barrels per day (BPD) in June.

✅ Higher exports come as several domestic refineries remain idle, increasing crude availability for overseas shipments.

✅ The surge in exports could influence global crude supply dynamics and keep a close watch on oil price movements.

✅ Energy markets may remain volatile as traders assess the impact on global demand-supply balance.
